item.title: Visualizing my journey to Inbox Zero
item.description:
31 March 2020

I subscribe to the “Inbox Zero” philosophy and treat my email inbox as a todo list that I slowly work through. As part of the desire to get more and more quantitative I wrote a quick script to pull the number of emails from my Inbox and then insert the data as a row into a new table in my personal stats database. As usual, most of the work was in deciding to do it and once I got to coding the hacky solution was done within 20 minutes. The script uses Python’s built-in imaplib library to log in to an email provider and then a simple MySQL query to insert the resulting data. I hooked this up to run every 15 minutes via cron and put together a Grafana dashboard to plot the count over time. I’m currently not actually going through the content of the email messages themselves but there are tons of directions I can take this - for example slicing the data by sender or examining the age of the messages. For now I’m just hopeful this motivates me to keep going through that email.

#! /usr/bin/python3

import imaplib
import mysql.connector

# TODO: Handle credentials better (environment, config, etc)
IMAP_INFO = {
    'work': {
        'imap_host': '',
        'imap_port': 993,
        'imap_user': '',
        'imap_pass': '',
    },
    'personal': {
        'imap_host': '',
        'imap_port': 993,
        'imap_user': '',
        'imap_pass': '',
    },
}

db_host = ''
db_user = ''
db_pass = ''
db_database = ''

def get_num_emails(creds):
    imap = imaplib.IMAP4_SSL(creds['imap_host'], creds['imap_port'])
    imap.login(creds['imap_user'], creds['imap_pass'])
    imap.select('Inbox')
    tmp, data = imap.search(None, 'ALL')
    return len(data[0].split())

def save_stats(account_name, num_emails):
    mydb = mysql.connector.connect(
        host = db_host,
        user = db_user,
        passwd = db_pass,
        database = db_database,
    )
    mycursor = mydb.cursor()

    sql = "INSERT INTO email_stats (account_name, num_emails) VALUES (%s, %s)"
    val = (account_name, num_emails)
    mycursor.execute(sql, val)
    mydb.commit()
    print(mycursor.rowcount, "record inserted.")

for account_name, creds in IMAP_INFO.items():
    num_emails = get_num_emails(creds)
    print('Fetched', num_emails, 'in', account_name)
    save_stats(account_name, num_emails)

item.title: Bridging gap of Bayesian/story-comparison models of juridical inference (2013)
item.description:
Abstract

In this article, I offer a survey and critique of the long-standing debate within evidence scholarship over what descriptive model best captures the manner in which judges and juries determine disputed facts. For decades, there has been a stand-off between Bayesians, who believe that the task of fact-finding can best be understood in probabilistic terms, and story-model proponents, who describe the task of fact-finding in terms of comparative evaluation of competing stories. Drawing on the dual-process tradition in psychology and cognitive science research, I argue that committing to either view on its own fails to capture important aspects of the fact-finding process. The dual-process tradition predicts that judges and juries typically process a great volume of information in an automatic and unconscious way that bears a closer resemblance to the dense networks of conditional probability relations envisioned by Bayesians than to the linear comparisons of separate, hypothetical stories. At the same time, however, fact-finders sometimes engage a second layer of conscious, semantically based reasoning that does involve explicit story-comparison. Sometimes such reasoning is used to channel or override the intuitions that flow from the first stage of reasoning, but at other times it will work instead to justify or defend such intuitions from outside attack. This dual-process account has important implications for the debate between Bayesian and story-model approaches, making it more likely that components of both are necessary to fully appreciate the task of fact-finding.

1. Introduction

For more than four decades, evidence scholars have debated whether probabilistic or story-comparison models provide a better way to conceptualize the task of fact-finding in legal disputes. These models can be used for different purposes, so the terms of the discussion vary accordingly. Sometimes the focus is normative, and the question is on identifying the inference procedures that produce optimal trial outcomes, either in terms of accuracy or some other relevant metric. A related, but distinct, inquiry explores the extent to which existing fact-finding procedures can or should incorporate statistical, econometric, epidemiological, or other formal models as aids to good decision-making. A third inquiry is doctrinal, and the focus is on identifying a model that best fits and justifies the existing matrix of legal rules that regulate the fact-finding process. Finally, there is a long-standing debate is over which approach serves as the best descriptive model for conceptualizing the inferential work that legal fact-finders perform over the course of a typical trial. This article addresses this final debate, and suggests that the dual-process tradition in cognition research provides a way to move past the long-opposed Bayesian and story-model accounts and to more accurately describe the task of fact-finding. My focus will be on fact-finding as performed by judges and juries in adversarial trials, whether civil or criminal in nature, although this discussion may also provide insights regarding fact-finders who operate in inquisitorial or administrative settings.

One longstanding school of thought—often identified as ‘Bayesian’ by reference to its preference for a subjective interpretation of probability and its use of Bayes’ Theorem as an analytic tool—has viewed the internal process of deliberating about facts as a series of internal probability adjustments propagating through a vast network of inferential connections. In this view, a fact-finder identifies a number of key factual questions that are being disputed in a case early in the fact-finding process. Each new piece of evidence either increases or decreases the degree to which the fact-finder believes that each key fact in question is demonstrated or refuted. The fact-finder’s changing degree of conviction can be modelled using traditional probability notation, so that it varies between 0—where a fact has been proven false beyond any doubt—and 1, where a fact has been proven true beyond any doubt. Over the life of a case, jurors will make innumerable updates to their degree of belief regarding what facts have, or have not, been shown. Then, after all the evidence has been presented, they will choose the winner and loser by applying legal rules to those probable facts. The Bayesian model, therefore, envisions fact-finding as the construction of an inferential network of conditional probability relations, from which the ultimate probabilities of legal interest are computed.

An alternative approach, sometimes known as the ‘Story Model’, offers a rival way of understanding the process of fact-finding. In this framing, judges and jurors do not, and could not, independently calculate the likelihood of each disputed fact in a case. Instead, they evaluate and compare the broader stories offered by the parties in a case, and ask which is a better explanation of the evidence they have seen. Factual support plays a large role in this evaluation, but the coherence of each parties’ overall narrative and its consistency with the fact-finder’s background beliefs are also important. In place of the myriad internal probability updates in the Bayesian model, story-theorists offer instead an imaginary scale on which two accounts are balanced, with the more acceptable being chosen without any inquiry into the separate probability of its many constitutive factual elements.

I urge that a full picture of fact-finding should incorporate features of both models. A growing wealth of evidence from experimental psychology and cognitive science reveals that human cognition involves two types of information processing working side-by-side. One type of cognition operates rapidly, effortlessly, and unconsciously. This ‘System 1’ reasons in an associative manner, in which new sensory data automatically give rise to beliefs, actions, and emotions, without the need for conscious processing. In contrast, ‘System 2’ processes are slow, effortful, and conscious. Instead of automatic associative processing, System 2 processing can invoke explicitly learned semantic rules, with the steps of cognition drawing on a limited resource of working memory and subject to conscious awareness. System 2 is capable of restraining the impulses that flow from System 1, but we perceive this form of cognition as effortful. Consequently, we often rely on intuitions arising from System 1 even when it produces answers that we would not endorse on more careful reflection.

Turning back to the debate over heuristic models of fact-finding, this dual-process approach to understanding cognition has surprising pay-offs. One is that a better conception of fact-finding would incorporate both Bayesian and story-model components. Perhaps counter-intuitively, the fast, associative processing style of System 1 bears a far closer connection to the complex internal networks of probability updating envisioned by Bayesians than it does to the sequential, semantic, and reasoned vision of the Story Model. That this has escaped notice in the debate over fact-finding models may be largely due to the fact that System 1 processing lies below the threshold of conscious attention, meaning that its operation is not available to introspection. But System 1 processing is not the whole story; when System 2 processes are invoked, our conscious minds can restrain the initial impulses of our vast associative unconscious, and such processing often operates in a serial, linguistic fashion. One form that such processing can take involves explicit comparisons between competing stories, framed as hypothetical explanations of the given evidence. Some aspects of System 2 functioning, therefore, may be better understood by using the story-model view than the Bayesian framework.

Thus, closer attention to the cognitive architecture that real-world fact-finders employ suggests that juridical inference involves both a rapid and automatic process of updating a large number of internal belief registers as well as a restraining impulse that resolves factual disputes by considering them in serial, linguistic terms. In other words, the task of fact-finding involves both probabilistic updating and the evaluation of story coherence and plausibility.

2. Bayesian inferential networks

A Bayesian approach to understanding the task of fact-finding begins with a simple premise, which is that the questions we try to answer through the trial process involve inherent and unavoidable uncertainty. It will be a rare case indeed when a judge or jury can say that guilt or innocence has been shown with certainty. This problem arises because the task of fact-finding involves historical reconstruction of events that the decision-maker has not witnessed personally, and because key facts may have gone unobserved or else will be reported by witnesses whose veracity and testimonial capacity can never be known beyond doubt. Thus, although the law regularly demands binary, ‘black or white’ answers—in terms of guilt versus innocence, or liability versus none—such decisions must rest upon uncertain factual inferences which exist only in varying shades of gray.

For Bayesian theorists, this bedevilling uncertainty suggests that if we wish to understand fact-finding, we would be well-served to employ the mathematical tools of probability analysis. The Bayesian approach differs from the frequentist interpretation of probability theory, which conceives of probabilities as objective descriptions of the likelihood of getting certain results when sampling randomly from fixed distributions, such as by drawing playing cards from shuffled decks (Oaksford and Chater, 2007). This objective interpretation of probability seems to have little application to legal fact-finding, which involves singular encounters with unique crimes. Bayesians, in contrast, use probability theory to model ‘subjective’ probabilities, by which they mean our varying degrees of internal confidence in particular propositions (Kaye, 1979). By ranking our internal barometer of confidence in particular beliefs from 0 (where the belief is false beyond any doubt), through 0.5 (where we feel completely uncertain whether the belief is true or false), to 1 (where it is certain beyond any doubt), Bayesianism invites us to model the process of analyzing evidence using the well-elaborated tools of formal probability theory.

We can use subjective probability theory to describe both the overall task of fact-finding, as well as how a fact-finder responds over time to new pieces of evidence in a disputed case, using Bayes’ rule for evaluating conditional probabilities (Kadane and Schum, 1996). Bayes’ rule addresses the common phenomenon where we must decide how much to change our degree of confidence in an uncertain proposition (H), such as whether a defendant (let us call him Daniel) stabbed a victim (Victor), based upon encountering a particular piece of evidence (e), such as learning that a blood-stained knife was found in the defendant’s house. In Bayesian terms, we call this the problem of determining what ‘posterior probability’ we should give to the hypothesis that Daniel stabbed Victor, which requires assessing how much impact the new evidence should have on our preexisting assessment of the ‘prior probability’ of that hypothesis. In such scenarios, Bayes’ rule provides a method for calculating the posterior probability given the new evidence:  

In other words, our posterior confidence in our hypothesis of guilt H given the new item of evidence e, P(H|e), is a function of (1) our prior confidence in H before learning about e, P(H); (2) the level of confidence we would have in e if we were sure that H was true, P(e|H); and (3) the level of confidence we would have in e if we were sure that H was false, P(e|not-H). So, in the case of our potential knife-wielding assassin Daniel, we would need to combine our prior estimate of Daniel’s guilt with the odds that Daniel would have a bloody knife in his home either if he was innocent or if he was guilty, to produce our new estimate of the probability of his guilt.

Bayes’ rule provides us with both a holistic way to model the overall trial process as well as a way of thinking about evolution of a fact-finder’s beliefs over the course of a trial. The overall process of fact-finding can be understood as an evaluation of the probability of guilt versus innocence based on a pre-trial prior probability, a probability that the existing matrix of evidence would have been produced in the event of guilt, and the probability that the same matrix would have resulted if the defendant were innocent. (The same could be said of the determination of liability in a civil trial, although I will discuss only the criminal example from here forward for the sake of simplicity.) There is some attendant difficulty in specifying what the pre-trial prior probability ought to be, but this should not be seen as fatal when we are using this as a descriptive, rather than a normative, model, because it seems quite clear that a fact-finder has some level of confidence in the guilt of an accused person before a trial begins, even if we cannot agree what that level ought to be.

The model grows both more complicated, and more powerful, when we expand to considering the many changes to a fact-finder’s confidence levels that are likely to occur over the course of a trial. It might seem initially attractive to imagine that a trial is simply an iterated series of simple Bayesian updates to a floating probability of guilt. But this will not do; it seems clear that fact-finders can not directly connect each piece of evidence to such ultimate conclusions. Thus, imagine briefly that Daniel has testified that his knife was bloody because he had used it to butcher some meat and neglected to wash it, and has also introduced testimony from a friend (Frank) that he was in another city on the day of the crime. In our hypothetical trial, fact-finders will most likely be weighing more probabilities than simply ‘what is the likelihood that Daniel has murdered Victor’; they also will be asking, ‘How likely is it that Frank is a truthful person?’ or ‘How likely is it that Daniel was cooking that day?’ An astute observer would notice that the two probabilities tend to cancel each other out: It is less likely that Daniel was cooking in his own kitchen if he was out of town on the same day! And that helps to illustrate a larger problem, which is that evaluating the impact of a new piece of evidence on the varying probabilities that a fact-finder must determine involves, not just assessing the independent force of that evidence in the areas of its immediate and obvious relevance (i.e. ‘What are the odds that Daniel killed Victor given testimony that he was out of town?’) but also assessing its impact on the larger network of existing probability assessments (i.e. ‘What effect does that testimony have on the prior network of inferences on which our pre-existing determinations depend?’).

Some proponents of Bayesian models of trial inference respond to this difficulty by imagining the process, not as an iterated series of updating calculations, but rather as the construction and elaboration of inference networks over time. Drawn from Wigmore’s use of charts to describe the related inferences that could be drawn from evidence at trial, Kadane and Schum (1996) describe the process of incorporating new items of evidence into the probabilistic calculus as one of adding nodes to a directed acyclic graph, in which each new piece of evidence might conceivably adjust the likelihood of numerous intermediate nodes. In this framing, fact-finders amass a network of dependency relationships between individual evidence items and various hypotheses about past events over the course of a trial. If the trial was to be paused at any point, the partially constructed network could, in theory, be used to produce confidence estimates for each existing node and for the ultimate determination of guilt or innocence. Ultimately, however, the goal is to produce a network that embodies all the relevant evidence that the parties have presented, which should then produce a posterior probability of guilt that gives appropriate weight to all of that evidence.

Bayesian fact-finding models have been subjected to stringent attack for nearly four decades. Some of these critiques involve attempting to show that a competing model is superior, and will be considered later. But a few attempt to show that a process of probabilistic updating in a network of inference relationships is an inherently unworkable approach for understanding fact-finding inference.

Allen (1997) has argued that Bayesian networks involve an implausible quantity of computation to be useful as models of real-world fact-finding. As discussed above, each new piece of evidence might conceivably interact with every other probability estimate previously constructed before producing a final result. What is worse, some interactions might only become apparent when the new piece of evidence was compared with various combinations of previously considered possibilities, meaning that a fully complete Bayesian update would require considering the interaction of each new piece of evidence with all subsets of the total array of prior probability estimates. Granted, many of these checks will lead swiftly to the conclusion that there is no plausible interaction between the new evidence and most of the preceding facts of the case, but this is only knowable once the check has been performed, and sheer combinatorics imply that, for each n pieces of evidence, 2n checks will be required. Even a highly simplified case involving 30 pieces of evidence would therefore involve more than a billion separate conditional probability estimations. Allen sees such ‘combinatorial explosions’ as demonstrating the inherent absurdity of using Bayes’ rule as a complete model of the factual inference that must occur over the life of a trial, although he acknowledges that the rule may sometimes be useful as an aid to decision-making when fact-finders are evaluating a small quantity of explicitly statistical evidence.

A related critique points out that human beings do not naturally conform their explicit probability estimations to Bayes’ rule. A well-studied example involves asking experimental subjects to evaluate DNA-based identification evidence with statistically quantified error rates (Shklar and Diamond, 1999). In a series of experiments, test subjects were provided with sample trial evidence and asked to quantify their belief in guilt on a probabilistic scale; they were then exposed to DNA identification evidence that had an explicit probabilistic component, in the form of a random-match probability (RMP). The RMP figure indicates the likelihood that a series of comparisons between samples from two different individuals would match by random chance alone. In a series of experiments, subjects repeatedly failed to conform their posterior probability estimates to what Bayes’ rule would require, and this difference persisted even when some training was offered regarding Bayesian norms (Smith et al., 1996). This seems to bode poorly for the prospects of Bayesian networks as a model for fact-finding: If ordinary people fail to draw inferences in a way that the rule would predict even when presented with explicit probabilities and when instructed on the rules’ operation, how likely is it that they are conforming their estimates to the rule when operating in the more complex and murky realm of real-world trials?

One further critique of the Bayesian model is commonly termed the ‘reference class’ problem. We might wish to ground our subjective confidence estimates in some real-world frequency data, in order to avoid the charge that we are applying Bayes’ rule to meaningless numbers. But when we try to do so, we will quickly discover that it is hard to meaningfully describe ‘objective’ conditional probabilities in disputed cases. Allen and Pardo (2007) provide a number of useful examples of this problem in legal analysis, including the following: Imagine that a carpet fibre is found at the scene of a crime, and it is matched to a carpet in the home of a suspect. How do we determine the likelihood that the match is a coincidence? In theory, we might wish to look to the frequency of such fibres in some sample of carpets—but what is the relevant sample? Do we look at all carpets manufactured in the world? In the USA? All carpets sold in the same town that the victim lives in? All carpets owned by people with similar demographic characteristics to the suspect? Even if we had easy access to such information, there may be no non-arbitrary way to select the appropriate reference class by which to determine the ‘base rate’ of carpet fibre matches for people ‘like’ the suspect. This might imply that it will not be possible to match the subjective probabilities that we employ in a Bayesian network analysis with any objective, frequency-based probabilities arising out of the facts of the case.1 For some critics, this makes Bayesian networks a bad model for describing juridical inference.

3. Models based on story comparison

As an alternative to the Bayesian network approach, a number of scholars have urged that fact-finding should be envisioned instead as a comparative process, in which fact-finders evaluate competing historical narratives, or ‘stories’, and choose the one that has the best fit with both the available evidence and the fact-finder’s own background beliefs. Ergo, these theorists described their ‘story model’ approach as ‘explanation-based’, rather than probabilistic in nature (Pennington and Hastie, 1988). Unlike the Bayesian approach, which had its roots in mathematical formalizations of reasoning about uncertain events, the story model approach grew out of two primary sources. One inspiration for this approach came from psychological investigations into the experience of decision-making by mock jurors (Pennington and Hastie, 1992). In addition, this tradition found additional support from theories of abductive inference in epistemology and the philosophy of science (Josephson, 2001).

Pennington and Hastie’s work with mock jurors provided the early foundations for the story model. They asked experimental subjects to watch filmed trials and then interviewed them to elicit descriptions of the reasoning process that the subjects used to arrive at a verdict decision. They found that their mock jurors explained their decisions by referencing stories they had constructed, which blended both some pieces of evidence in the case with other assumptions that they used to fill in temporal or causal gaps in their narratives (Pennington and Hastie, 1986). Such stories, they argue, are constructed spontaneously when jurors encounter evidence, and they contribute causally to a juror’s verdict decision. When conflicting stories are possible, jurors will choose between them on the basis of the stories’ overall plausibility, but a candidate story’s attractiveness will also depend upon the ease with which a parties’ evidence presentation can be construed in narrative form.

These researchers argue that the story model, and the experimental results that support it, are incompatible with the idea of Bayesian inference networks (Pennington and Hastie, 1992). In one experiment, they showed that presenting additional information that made one possible story more consistent had a greater impact on verdicts than it did on any individual probability judgment. What is more, when jurors were asked both to provide a story-based account of their understanding of the facts, and also to rank various factual judgments in explicitly probabilistic terms, their story structures better predicted their verdicts than did a researcher-computed Bayesian calculation based on their separate probability estimates.

Another group of theorists, who connect story-based reasoning with the idea of abductive inference, argue that a model of story-comparison avoids some of the problems that plague a Bayesian account of fact-finding. Abductive inference, as contrasted with the more familiar inferential forms of induction and deduction, involves trying to locate a hypothesis that will best explain available data, and so has also been called ‘inference to the best explanation’ or IBE. This basic structure, which involves reasoning from observables to unobservable underlying possibilities, has much in common with legal fact-finding, which is similarly devoted to reconstructing a historical truth from trial testimony and evidence. For this and other reasons, theorists such as Josephson (2001), Thagard (2004), and Pardo and Allen (2008) have proposed that we should conceptualize the fact-finding process as a choice among possible hypotheses, presented by contending parties, that explain the evidence offered at trial and produce divergent legal consequences. By doing so, they argue, we may be able to avoid some of the difficulties that have been associated with probabilistic accounts of proof, such as the combinatorial explosion, the theories’ alleged psychological implausibility, or the difficult problems with identifying appropriate reference classes to support conditional probability estimates.

Despite these attractive features, however, story-comparison accounts of fact-finding suffer from their own difficulties. Some have charged that basing real-life verdict decisions on the attractiveness of competing stories injects an excessive likelihood of prejudice and bias into a process that should, ideally, be more objective (Anderson et al., 2005). This critique is of great concern to those who would deploy story-comparison frameworks as a normative guide to rational proof, and thus a recent trend in story-comparison modelling involves an effort to show how this choice might be made in a rational or objective way. Thus, for instance, Bex has articulated a ‘hybrid’ model that incorporate a networked analysis of evidential support into the process of story choice (Bex, 2011), while Amaya has tried to identify hallmarks of virtuous epistemic behaviour that would indicate when story choice is likely to be defensible (Amaya, 2008).

But whether or not such measures can salvage story-comparison models from the charge that such models incorporate too much bias to be normatively optimal is an entirely separate question from that being considered here, which is whether they are more descriptively accurate than competing alternatives. On that score, the bias critique seems quite weak. First, it is hard to square the operation of Bayesian networks with any objective criteria for selecting conditional probabilities, so that theory may not have any significant advantage in terms of its objectivity. Second, and more importantly for the purpose of descriptive modelling of trial inference, it is simply a non-sequitur to object to a descriptive model on the grounds that it sometimes predicts outcomes that are normatively undesirable. Few would doubt that fact-finding is sometimes tainted by bias and prejudice, so the fact that a descriptive model might allow those factors to play a causal role in determining verdicts is hardly a damning blow.

A stronger objection was raised by Schum (2001), which is that we seem to lack settled criteria for deciding which among competing explanations is ‘best’. Story model proponents have pointed to a variety of factors that justify choosing one model over another. Among relevant factors, theorists have listed the quantity of evidence that is explained by a theory, the coherence of a story (both internally and with our background beliefs), and its simplicity (with simpler stories being preferable to more complicated ones) (Pennington and Hastie, 1992; Thagard, 2004; Pardo and Allen, 2008). But what seems to be absent is any suggestion of how to choose among competing principles when they conflict, or a procedure for defining coherence that avoids ad hoc choices by whoever is engaged in applying a story model to a real or hypothetical trial. In other words, just as Bayesianism is subject to the critique that it fails to anchor subjective probabilities in criteria that can be objectively ascertained, so too are explanatory accounts guilty of hand-waving when they address why some explanations are better than others. Ultimately, both accounts, in order to be implemented, require a modeller to imagine herself in the fact-finder’s position and simulate what conclusions she would reach in that circumstance. If the story-model boils down to asserting that ‘fact-finders will prefer the account of the case that they find to be a more preferable explanation of the evidence’, without giving content to what makes some stories ‘preferable,’ it risks becoming tautological.

Another important critique was offered by Lempert (1988). He charged that story comparison frameworks have difficulty in accounting for the fact that many legal systems will reject a plaintiff’s case, or find a criminal defendant not guilty, without performing any comparisons whatsoever. In the former situation, we have a well-settled practice of granting summary judgment or directed verdicts in cases where the plaintiff’s case is insufficiently convincing on its own, without any explicit comparison to a counter-theory offered by the defendant. Similarly, sometimes criminal juries refuse to convict a defendant who has not offered any theory of innocence, but who has instead laboured merely to show the weakness of the government’s proof of his guilt. In all these circumstances, the problem seems to be that the cardinal probability that the plaintiff’s story is correct is insufficiently convincing to rise above a given threshold. But admitting of such reasoning seems to draw us back towards a model devoted to assessing whether the party with the burden of proof has shown their case beyond a specified level of probability, rather than merely offered a better story than the other side.

4. An introduction to dual-process models

It is possible that this long-standing debate has come to a standstill precisely because each model captures an important component of fact-finding inference, but neither by itself can adequately describe that process. It is my suggestion that a dual-process theory of fact-finding cognition, based in large part on the more general dual-process theories that have been developed by cognitive scientists and psychologists to explain certain puzzling features of human reasoning, would in fact combine attractive features of both models while explaining some features of legal fact-finding that the Bayesian and the story-model approaches cannot.

There is now a dominant consensus among cognitive scientists that ‘the functioning of the brain can be characterized by two different types of cognition having somewhat different functions and different strengths and weaknesses’ (Stanovich, 2011). Since the 1970s, a variety of research programmes in psychology and cognitive science have produced a consistent pattern of results. On a wide variety of cognitive tasks, people engage in two, sometimes conflicting, modes of information processing. On some tasks, answers come quickly, effortlessly, and automatically, while at other times, we consciously experience a slow, effortful process of reasoning to a conclusion. Increasingly, researchers have come to identify such differences as indicative of two different modes of reasoning and judgment, working side by side in our minds, sometimes collaboratively and sometimes in conflict with each other. These two competing modes of reasoning are commonly referred to as ‘System 1’ and ‘System 2’ (e.g. Frankish and Evans, 2009; Guthrie et al., 2008; Stanovich and West, 2000). By this terminology, researchers seek to express the view that various cognitive processes naturally fall into one of two modes or styles of information processing, and not, in contrast, the view there are two physically separated substructures within the brain (Kahneman, 2011; Stanovich, 2011).

When we rely primarily on System 1 processes to solve a problem or engage in a task, we experience no mental effort, only the rapid recognition of a seemingly correct result or the execution of actions without conscious planning. A classic example of System 1 at work occurs when we recognize a known face in a crowd—despite the intense amount of mental processing that is inherent in detecting the minute differences in appearance between the known face and the many unknown faces, we experience no internal effort, just a flash of recognition. Likewise, when we read a series of printed words on a page in a language we understand fluently, we must apply a great number of learned rules in order to make sense of them, but we usually experience no felt effort. Similarly, when walking or driving along a well-known route, we may handle many of the details of stopping, starting, or avoiding other drivers without noticing that we are making any decisions; indeed, our mind can be occupied entirely by other tasks without disrupting our ability to perform a complex series of steps. Nor is such automatic and fluid ease of effort associated only with ‘simple’ or ‘ordinary’ tasks—an expert chess player may experience rapid flashes of intuition regarding which positions are good and which are bad that are similarly effortless. Rather, the common feature of System 1 processing is the acquisition of judgment or execution skills through practice and feedback loops, and the ability to execute those skills with fluid, automatic ease (Smith and DeCoster, 2000).

In contrast, when we use System 2 to solve problems or make decisions, we consciously experience going through a series of mental steps and perceive those steps as cognitively effortful. The operations involved in adding two three-digit numbers together are far simpler, in any objective sense, than those involved in understanding an English language sentence, but aside from a few savants, we must go through a series of conscious, mentally taxing steps to arrive at an answer. These are signals that we are using System 2 rather than System 1. System 2 processes usually involve either the focus of attention on particular tasks, active restraint of a felt desire to engage in particular action, or the processing of verbal information or images that are held in working memory. Thus, when we think abstractly, apply explicit rules to analyze a situation, scan our memories for hard-to-recall information, memorize an unfamiliar sentence, or learn a new skill, we experience the effortful involvement of System 2 (Kahneman, 2011). One interesting aspect of System 2 is that the activities it facilitates can be learned explicitly, even without the lengthy practice needed to train the automatic actions of System 1. Because it draws on working memory and involves focused attention, System 2 allows us to modify our behaviour based on explicit instructions or ideas, and to learn faster than would be possible if we were dependent on System 1 alone (Smith and DeCoster, 2000).

One key contrast between System 1 and System 2 is that the former appears to work primarily via associative, parallel processing, while the latter can make use of semantic rules applied in a serial fashion (Smith and DeCoster, 2000). System 1’s associative processing seems to have its roots in the basic connectionist architecture of our neural networks, in which separate words, concepts, and ideas develop excitatory or inhibitory links over time. These links are strengthened when separate neural representations are triggered together, and atrophy in the absence of co-occurrence, thus causing our associative networks to broadly mirror the patterns we encounter in our lives. What is more, these networks can link ideas with preferences; when certain sensory information tends to be associated with pleasant or unpleasant results, we develop emotional responses to the associated stimuli. This connectionist architecture of thought is frequently described as an ancient feature of our brains, one that we share in common with a wide array of non-human animals (Evans, 2008). In contrast, the reasoning of System 2 seems to proceed in a linear, sequential fashion, allowing us to apply newly learned explicit rules without going through a lengthy process of learning by experience or practice. System 2 also has a unique capability that System 1 mostly lacks: the ability to use and manipulate hypothetical scenarios that are kept separate from our representations of the real world (Stanovich, 2011).

One truly fascinating aspect of the dual-process tradition is its treatment of the interactions between the two systems of cognition. A few broad points on this topic are worth mentioning. First, System 2 processes have the capacity to override the initial responses of System 1 (Stanovich and West, 2000; Wilson et al., 2000). A wide variety of reasoning experiments have asked subjects to solve problems in which an initial, but wrong, answer is likely to arise from associative processing; such answers will ‘feel right’ so long as System 2 does not attend too closely to what is going on. But if subjects are prompted, by various means, to slow down and think through the problem in a linear fashion, such errors are much reduced.

Second, the unfortunate flip side of this insight is that we often fail to activate System 2 processing even when it might be useful. Part of the problem is that using System 2 places demands on working memory and involves focusing our attention, both of which we experience as fatiguing. This means that engaging and sustaining System 2 involvement requires motivation and willpower. Thus, people who are ego-depleted will find it harder to restrain their associative intuitions and impulses through analytical reasoning processes (Hagger et al., 2010). What is more, if an answer suggested by System 1 is appealing, we may lack the motivation necessary to engage in further effortful processing to confirm that result using System 2. This helps to explain the well-documented phenomenon of ‘belief bias’, in which people are less likely to notice logical errors in statements that they find otherwise agreeable than they are to notice similar errors in statements they find objectionable (Evans, 2008).

Finally, one complicating aspect of the dual-process model is that although System 2 can sometimes restrain System 1, it will on other occasions work to justify the results of System 1 processing even when those results might not survive neutral reflection (Haidt, 2001). For instance, subjects who were primed to feel disgust when reading random words were more likely to describe neutral vignettes as involving morally wrong behaviour. Nevertheless, despite the fact that their condemnation was actually caused by the unconscious associative action of their System 1 rather than by the content of the story, some participants used moral reasoning to justify the intuitions supplied by the associative activity of System 1 processes (Wheatley and Haidt, 2005). Thus, activating System 2 will not necessarily suffice to restrain problematic System 1 responses; it is also necessary to supply an appropriate motivation to use System 2 for reflective rather than justificatory purposes.

5. Dual-process fact-finding

There are several important lessons that scholars of legal fact-finding can draw from the dual-process tradition. When we incorporate System 1 processing into our view of how fact-finders interact with evidence, some of the terrain over which Bayesians and story-modellers have fought seems to lose its relevance. Nor is this interesting only to those who wish to keep running tallies of the points scored in an old academic war. Rather, thinking of fact-finding in dual-process terms helps to explain several features of American trials that are harder to justify using either of the old models by itself. What is more, thinking of fact-finding in dual-process terms also suggests important research questions for future evidence scholarship.

A brief sketch of dual-process fact-finding runs as follows: As a judge or juror listens to evidence in court, or reads evidence presented in documentary form, most of the processing that occurs is implicit and pre-conscious, within the domain of System 1. The fact-finder understands the words that are spoken using automatic language-processing systems, and links the parties in the case to various associative tokens in an automatic way. As past events are described and characterized by witnesses, new associative tokens will be built and integrated into the larger network of associations that is implicit in the networked structure of our minds. Some connections will be stronger than others, and the weight and pattern of connections can be said to be a set of implicit, pre-conscious beliefs about the past facts that gave rise to the case.

As the case progresses, various associations will be suggested both by witness testimony and lawyer argument. Indeed, given that System 1 is likely to form new connections automatically, there is little reason to think that testimony will be significantly privileged over non-testimonial associations that arise based on attorneys’ questions and summarizing statements. Not all of these suggestions will be reflected in the internal associational scheme developed by the fact-finder, however. Two primary factors might prevent a fact-finder from constructing an associative scheme based on a witness’s (or lawyer’s) assertion. First, the witness might not be viewed as trustworthy; this could be due to effective impeachment or to more arbitrary factors such as the witness’s appearance or background. Second, the witness’s assertion might not integrate well into the fact-finder’s pre-existing associative matrix. This matrix will have been constructed by the pattern of co-occurring activations of mental schema over the course of the fact-finder’s life. Sometimes these patterns will closely map onto real world tendencies for event co-variance, so that fact-finders accept likely testimony and disregard implausible accounts. Thus, if a fact-finder lived in a jurisdiction with generally honest and trustworthy law enforcement officers, and had formed positive associations based on that experience, even an otherwise trustworthy witness might provoke instinctive distrust if he suggested that the authorities had conspired to frame an innocent individual.

At other times, however, the fact-finder’s network of associative weights will be skewed by various social factors that can cause our internal barometers of likelihood to diverge from real-world patterns of events. This can arise for a number of reasons. For one thing, associative weights will have been developed largely based on the frequency with which various events co-occurred. But if news or social gossip tends to focus excessively on certain patterns of conduct out of proportion to its actual likelihood, then a fact-finder might be improperly pre-disposed to accept stories that integrate with that falsely derived sense of frequency. Thus, for example, if news reports are more likely to report incidents of stranger rape than acquaintance rape, fact-finders may be more reluctant to accept the story of a victim who claims to have been raped by someone she knows than is justifiable based on real world frequencies because the reported combination of events does not map neatly to the fact-finders internal schema for rape. Perhaps even more worryingly, we can develop implicit conceptual associations from fiction as well as from fact. Thus, if films and TV regularly cast members of a certain racial group as villains, we may be over-likely to accept accounts that fit in with such fictional schema and reluctant to trust stories that cast them as innocents. And because System 1 operates below the level of our conscious attention, this may occur even if we would consciously reject the stereotypes embodied in our underlying associative matrix.

Finally, the pattern of associative connections between case facts and concepts that develop over the course of a trial may also be influenced by various factors during the trial that would not typically be considered evidence. Thus, a lawyer who seems particularly honest and trustworthy might, via his association with his client, prime our associative systems to be more ready to trust the client’s own words when he speaks. Or, conversely, if the defendant is physically unattractive, our associative network might be primed to have a negative view of other, unrelated qualities, like his honesty or peacefulness, given the general linkages among positive and negative traits known as the ‘halo effect’, as demonstrated in experiments showing that mock jurors treat unattractive defendants more harshly (Spottswood, 2011; Rhode, 2009). Finally, hearing damaging information about a defendant’s past acts might well activate our associative machine to view them as a ‘bad person’ and therefore a person more likely to be guilty of a charged crime, even if we consciously believe that the evidence is irrelevant. So, in sum, System 1 does most of the cognitive work in a case by collating the wide variety of suggested concepts and constructing an implicit schema that relates them in an implicit network with the fact-finder’s pre-existing associative framework. This pattern of associative relations will sometimes be closely tied to the real world facts that gave rise to a case, but its associative structure may at other times lead fact-finders astray and contribute to intuitions about cases that are not justifiable.

Despite all of this talk of System 1, I do not mean to suggest that System 2 processing does not play an important role in fact-finding cognition. As the case progresses, fact-finders may be motivated or compelled to process this set of implicit beliefs by applying semantic rules or by walking through some subsets of the evidence in serial order. Sometimes this may arise due to an internal motivation, as when the facts of the case involve internal contradictions or are so confusing that the fact-finder has failed to form a clear picture of important events. At other times, the process itself may compel such processing, as when a judge must issue a written opinion, or when a juror must justify her beliefs to others during jury deliberations. Indeed, some aspects of the legal process involve new semantic rules that must be integrated with the associative model constructed by jurors. For instance, in applying a burden of persuasion, a juror is given a novel, semantic rule to apply in scenarios where her internal model is in conflict. In cases where the System 1 model has built up strong associations favouring or disfavouring a party, System 2 may play a primarily justificatory role if activated. However, if there are internal conflicts within the System 1 model such that it only weakly favors one side, encouraging reasoned System 2 deliberation may initiate a feedback process that could eventually result in an override of the initial tendency embodied in the associative model.

6. Implications of the dual-process approach for the debate between Bayesians and abductionists

How, then, does a dual-process view of fact-finding differ from prior models? Most significantly, it emphasizes that much of the cognitive labour involved in processing evidence lies below the threshold of conscious attention. Often when we listen to tales told by others, we do not experience any internal thoughts at all; rather, our attention is focused simply on the task of listening (or on various distractions that occur over time), and any model we form of the events being described is an implicit and pre-conscious pattern of System 1 associations. To the extent that such subconscious processes play an important role in fact-finding, modes of research that rely on the subjective reports of jurors will fail to capture these elements of the process or describe the internal logics they are employing. Indeed, we may run a risk of misunderstanding the real nature of most juridical reasoning when we rely on subjective reports. If story-telling is a natural mode of explaining facts to others, then an implicit organization of thought that has a very different structure might be translated into story structure when we ask a fact-finder to explain the basis of their beliefs. Given that such introspective reports provide some of the primary ammunition for those who contend that the story model is more descriptively accurate than the Bayesian alternative (e.g. Pennington and Hastie, 1986), this makes such arguments weaker than is commonly recognized.

In fact, thinking of fact-finding in dual-process terms may go a long way towards deflating the apparent tension between Bayesian and story-based models of evidential inference. Neural processing can be modeled as a quasi-Bayesian network, which incorporates prior probabilities in its internal structure and updates that structure in response to new evidence in a way that imperfectly tracks the formal demands of Bayes’ rule (Oaksford and Chater, 2007). Some object to this framing (e.g. Bowers and Davis, 2012), and few would view the correspondence as perfect, but any model must engage in reductive assumptions or it would not usefully simplify and characterize the phenomena it describes. Perhaps we could elaborate a version of a dual-process fact-finding model in which the System 1 operated as an approximately Bayesian inferential network, with prior probabilities fixed by past experience and the appropriate computations being implemented by the propagation and frequency of nodal activation in the network. (This could be the case even though subjects violate Bayes’ rule when asked to translate their internal confidence in numeric terms, because System 2 might not have full access to the real weights incorporated in the implicit structure of System 1.) Note, however, that the core insight here is not provided by the explicit conditional probability computations envisioned by Bayes’ rule, but rather by the notion of networked inferential updating that has been suggested as way to implement the rule during fact-finding. Other network-inspired inferential approaches, such as coherence networks (Thagard, 2004), might work as well or better at describing implicit fact-finding cognition. What is likely to fail, however, is an explicitly abductionist approach, involving linear comparison of separate, hypothetical stories. This is because Type-1 processing rarely involves the cognitive decoupling operations required to manipulate complex hypotheticals. Rather, it builds singular, focal models that are taken as true for purposes of action (Stanovich, 2011).

System 2, in contrast, would operate as an occasional constraint on this network, applying semantically structured rules or modes of reasoning that would in some cases produce different results than the ‘pure’ operation of System 1. The elaboration and comparison of serially structured, competing stories is one means by which such constraint could occur. When this occurs, fact-finding will indeed involve an explicitly abductionist component, even if such reasoning is only a part of a larger process. Other operations of System 2, however, will not always involve story-comparison. An example is the application of explicit standards of proof; such rules provide a fact-finder with an explicit rule (‘Find defendant guilty only if sure beyond a reasonable doubt’), which they apply to their implicit associative model. Such rule applications need not involve story comparison, but they are nevertheless a sign of System 2 involvement.

Standard complaints about the separate models seem to lose their force when they are integrated in this particular manner. Does pre-conscious, implicit factual inference implemented by the associative operation of a connectionist network involve an implausible combinatorial explosion of necessary computations? This is doubtful. What would be an unmanageable serial computation in a computer or a System 2 process can occur much more economically when implemented in the massively parallel architecture of System 1 (cf. Bechtel and Abrahamsen, 1991). What is more, connectionist networks can engage in what we could label ‘bounded Bayesianism’ to the extent that they do not connect all possible sub-sets of related conceptual clusters when processing evidence. Rather, many possible connections will simply not arise, due to lack of past associations and the fact that the parties and witnesses fail to suggest such connections. This, plus the fact that they can propogate nodal activation through the entire network of associations simultaneously, reduces the problem of propagating updates through a large network of related inferences to manageable size. Indeed, the complexly cross-linked structure of Bayesian inference networks actually makes them superior tools for imagining the operation of our vast associative network when compared with models involving a much smaller number of comparative evaluations.

Similarly, this sort of neural quasi-Bayesianism is not susceptible to the criticism that it requires the construction of reference classes without specifying the means for choosing the appropriate class. To the extent that proponents of an inference model try to claim that particular inferences in cases might come with objective conditional probabilities attached, the problem is a serious one. But because the dual-process approach would invoke instead the implicit, subjective probabilities formed by the sometimes skewed pattern of associative experiences in an individual fact-finder’s past and embodied in the structure of their System 1, we have identified both a particular reference class (the set of co-occurring token activations in a fact-finder’s past) and the reason why it is privileged over other possible classes. These reference classes will not always be normatively optimal, but this is not a stumbling block for a model whose goal is description of existing inference practices rather than perfection of them.

Furthermore, objections to the operation of story comparisons on the basis that we lack an objective basis for choosing winning and losing stories fall flat when we are considering those story comparisons within a descriptively oriented dual-process model. The dual-process model of fact-finding avoids invoking normatively laden language because it does not require that fact-finders will choose the ‘best’ or ‘most coherent’ among competing stories. Rather, to the extent they engage in story comparison, it will be based on the activation of System 2 in response to internally or externally inspired motivation, usually with explicit ends attached. Thus, a fact-finder might engage in story comparison in order to justify their associative intuitions in response to external pressure, or they might resolve internally conflicting intuitions by means of an externally supplied semantic burden-of-persuasion rule. With such differing goals, we should expect that the standards by which competing hypotheses will be judged will also vary based on the motivational goals of the fact-finder, regardless of whether the fact-finder is motivated to choose a story that prevails based on some objective metric.

Additionally, a dual-process model accommodates insufficiency of evidence cases without the difficulties inherent in a pure story-comparison model. If the sole available narrative does not mesh well with a fact-finder’s pre-existing associative schema, the party proposing it may not be able to activate the necessary association between the opposing party and the relevant category of wrong-doing. In such cases, the fact-finder will have the intuition that the party charged is innocent even though no hypothesis has been offered that explains the evidence in terms of innocence. This predicts, as a purely abductive model cannot, the legal system’s response to such cases, which is to reject the weak claim without requiring counter-proof.

7. Implications of a dual-process model for future evidence scholarship

A dual-process model of fact-finding, therefore, holds promise as a means of capturing many of the insights that make both Bayesian and story-comparison models attractive, while avoiding some of the difficulties that plague either approach in isolation. Before concluding, however, I wish to note one other feature of a two-system model of evidence processing, which is that it may help to provide convincing rationales for several features of evidence law that are harder to justify using the earlier approaches to modelling evidential inference. To the extent that the dual-process model can predict when judges and juries are likely to render illogical or biased decisions, it provides a meaningful advance over most prior articulations of the Bayesian and story-models.

Evidence law contains many rules designed to prevent juries from learning about certain forms of relevant information because of a fear that the evidence will be used in a way that will prejudice the accuracy of the trial’s outcome. Bayesian network conceptions of fact-finding do not predict when such restraint will be necessary, and indeed provide little help when trying to demarcate the line between relevant inferences and prejudicial ones. Likewise, story models offer little guidance as to why some stories that are simple or internally coherent would nonetheless be viewed as problematic if chosen by a jury. But the dual-process approach predicts that System 1 will, with some regularity, lead fact-finders to use evidence in way that is likely to lead to normatively sub-optimal outcomes, because their reasoning will be guided by associative networks with a structure derived not just from accurate information, but also from slanted reporting, deliberate priming, or even fiction. Moreover, it also suggests why merely informing jurors that they are likely to make mistakes will often not be sufficient; if the output of System 1 processes is strong enough, System 2 will be marshalled, not to override it, but to justify or defend System 1 intuitions from attack, and this may occur even if the fact-finder is subjectively convinced, at a conscious level, that a particular association is invidious. I do not claim that it will be possible to justify all exclusionary rules of evidence on this basis—some, indeed, rest clearly on foundations that have nothing to do with the accuracy of fact-finding—but many existing rules may make sense as a means of avoiding improper associations in the System 1 network.

Consider, for example, a common problem in murder prosecutions, which is the introduction of gory crime-scene photographs even in cases where they have little relevance, such as when the key issue is the identity of the perpetrator rather than the wrongfulness or nature of the act itself. Experiments involving mock jurors show that conviction rates rise significantly when such evidence is included, even when it lacks any apparent relevance to the determination of guilt (Simon, 2012). Although one can include an irrationally strong adjustment in guilt probabilities in a Bayesian network based on data like this, any such adjustment would be ad hoc and would not flow from any basic assumptions in the model. Likewise, although one can include biasing passions among the reasons why a party might choose one story over another (e.g. Thagard, 2003), the models themselves do not give much reason why anger aroused by crime scene photos would tend to support stories involving a charged defendant’s guilt more than some other individual’s. But the dual-process model, in contrast, can actually help us predict and understand why such evidence influences guilt judgments. As I discussed above, a common way of understanding System 1 processing focuses on its ability to automatically perceive and encode connections between co-occurring stimuli in our environment, without our awareness. This implies that, when shocking crime scene evidence is shown to a jury in the presence of the criminal defendant, that jury is unconsciously learning to associate that defendant with the victim, with the crime scene, and with violence. This association is not all powerful; it might be overridden by sufficiently strong evidence showing that the defendant was not present when the crime occurred. But in close cases, it will lead the jury to subtly privilege the conclusion that the defendant, rather than another person, committed the crime. The dual-process model, therefore, is capable of predicting and explaining prejudicial influences on jury decision-making that the older frameworks cannot, and can thereby provide guidance towards improving existing exclusionary rules in evidence law.

What is more, when such evidence cannot be excluded entirely, the dual-process model may also help us identify potential strategies for moderating its impact, such as by encouraging more careful deliberation or self-control as means of restraining a biased intuitive judgment. Modern trial procedure contains features that make sense as a means of trying to activate appropriate System 2 activity in order to encourage a more reflective mode of evidence processing. For instance, when problematic evidence cannot be excluded entirely, fact-finders are encouraged to try and disregard it in their deliberations; this is, in effect, an explicit plea for a System 2 override. Much research has been devoted to showing that such overrides rarely occur (Lieberman and Arndt, 2000), but the dual-process model also suggests that such laboratory results should be interpreted with caution. Subjects in a research setting may feel less motivation to restrain their intuitions then do jurors who are given such instructions by a sitting judge and who are aware that they are part of a process that wields a terrible power over the liberty of others.

These reflections are merely an early sketch of the potential power of dual-process models to help us both appreciate the virtues of our existing evidentiary rules and detect when they may do more harm than good. I include them to suggest that thinking of evidentiary inference as a sometimes-competitive interaction between two different modes of mental processing helps to explain why it is that we can both know that some evidence is problematic while worrying that explaining the problematic nature of that evidence to an intelligent fact-finder may not sufficiently resolve the problem. Neither a conception of Bayesian inference networks alone, nor an assumption that fact-finding involves primarily choosing the better among competing stories, should lead us to expect that such impasses will arise. But if an implicit associative network and explicit story evaluation sometimes point in different directions, it becomes natural to ask whether prejudice may arise where they conflict, and how we may avoid it.

8. Conclusion

In this article, I have suggested that the long debate over fact-finding models has suffered from a persistent failing. By relying overmuch on introspective experience to elucidate the structure of fact-finding inference, many theorists have neglected the important role that is played by the System 1 processes of associative inference, which lie below the threshold of conscious awareness. Although my reflections on the uses to which a two-system model of fact-finding can be put are quite preliminary, I hope that they illustrate the potentially great value of considering both the unconscious associative machine and its interaction with our parallel system of conscious, semantically structured information processing when we ponder the challenges of turning disputed evidence into legal and factual conclusions.

" 1 This problem may be resolvable in some cases via either the use of alternative priors as bounds (Gastwirth 2013) or by using statistical inference criteria to seek a normatively optimal balance between reference class size and specificity (Cheng 2009). Neither of these solutions, however, could realistically be applied to all the inference questions that might arise over the course of a real-world trial.

References

Allen
R J
. 
Rationality, Algorithms and Juridical Proof: A Preliminary Inquiry
, 
Int’l J. Evidence & Proof
, 
1997
, vol. 
1
 (pg. 
254
-
275
)
Allen

, 
Pardo
M S
. 
The Problemmatic Value of Mathematical Models of Evidence
, 
J. Legal Stud.
,

, vol.

 (pg.

-

)

item.title: iPhone 12 chipmaker TSMC on track for A-series chip production
item.description:
Apple chipmaker TSMC is reportedly on track to move forward with volume production of its next-gen 5-nanometer chips. This most likely refers to Apple’s next A-series chips for the iPhone 12 series of handsets.

According to a report published Wednesday, this production process is currently on as scheduled. This counters recent speculation that Apple had asked TSMC to defer manufacturing for the new iPhone.

A report published last week suggested that Apple has held internal discussions on whether to delay the launch of the 5G iPhone by several months. As with every iPhone in recent years, the iPhone 12 was expected to be released this fall. However, the report claimed that, due to fears about the economic conditions under which a new iPhone might launch, Apple is reportedly considering pushing this back.

Since then, several reports have pushed back at this. In a recent note to clients, analyst Gene Munster said that he expects Apple to be able to deliver its first 5G iPhone as planned. Printed circuit board suppliers in Apple’s supply chain have also dismissed rumors that they’ve been asked to delay volume production of the new iPhone.

TSMC: Bringing 5-nanometer tech to A-series chips

TSMC’s new A-series chip for the iPhone 12 is widely expected to take advantage of the new 5-nanometer process. The previous jump in terms of nanometers was Apple’s A12 Bionic chip in 2018, made using the 7-nanometer process. The iPhone was either the first or second phone in the world to include these chips. Although Huawei showed off its 7nm Kirin 980 first, Apple shipped its 7nm iPhones before Huawei. A recent report suggested the forthcoming A-series processor will be the first Arm-based mobile processor to exceed 3GHz.

Today’s Digitimes report isn’t the first time we’ve heard that TSMC manufacturing is on track. A report published by Digitimes in March said the company was preparing to “kick off” volume production of chips built using 5nm process technology in April. Although it didn’t name Apple as TSMC’s client, it said that process capacity has already been “fully booked” up.

Of course, if you can’t wait until September, there’s some good iPhone news on the horizon: A recent report suggests that the iPhone SE 2/iPhone could launch on April 15.

item.title: AMD Radeon 'Big Navi' RX Gamma Flagship GPU Specs and Benchmarks Leaked
item.description:
A featured image of the upcoming AMD 'Big' Navi GPU. Background image credits.

A massive leak has just sprung from an unprotected link on the website of an AMD partner site. It is also something that immediately puts into perspective a lot of the leaks we have been seeing so far. AMD's next-generation GPU will be known as Radeon RX Gamma XT. If you are thinking that this name has come out of nowhere then think again - because Gamma Cassiopeiae is the actual name of the star informally known as Navi. This ladies and gentlemen is the long-awaited Big Navi GPU.

AMD Radeon RX Gamma XT GPU: A 26 TFLOPs Monster With 1.5 TB/s of Bandwidth And 21.2 Gigarays Ray Tracing Support

The slides leaked appear to have been shared with AMD AIB and partners are represent the initial specification as well as an internal reference design for the shroud. From the looks of it, this is a dual-fan design of pretty much the same reference block as small Navi. That said, I am pretty sure this design is just for internal testing purposes (and not indicative of the final product) for now as AMD has already teased a much sexier looking design for big Navi.

A block diagram of the upcoming Gamma XT GPU has also been leaked. The compute engine capacity has been doubled and is referred to as QCU or Quad Compute Unit. What is (pleasantly) surprising is the fact that the card sustains a clock speed of 2.633 GHz - which is absolutely insane. The only reason a clock speed jump like this would be possible is that the card is manufactured on an EUV based 7nm process. TSMC was planning to roll out its 7nm EUV based process this year and it looks like AMD has managed to grab a piece of that pie.

Overall, the card contains 80 compute units. Since the slide mentions 5120 stream processors, we can now lay to rest rumors about an increase CU to SP ratio. It is still 64. The clock speeds are divided into 3 categories just like small Navi and the highest clock speed goes all the way up to 2633 MHz. Based on this clock speed and assuming the same instructions as the last architecture, this card can deliver a resounding 26.6 TFLOPs of single-precision compute. This is a phenomenal jump over the last generation and driven not only by an increase in core count, but by an increase in clock speeds as well.

It also looks like AMD has doubled down on ray tracing with support for up to 21.2 Gigarays per second. To put this into perspective, NVIDIA's current generation RTX flagship the RTX 2080 Ti can only sustain a Gigaray count of 10. This is more than twice that amount. What looks like a competitor of DLSS is also listed: "ClearAI upscaling". Depending on the price point, this could very well turn out to be the TITAN killer that all of us were waiting for.

The card will be based on a DisplayPort 2.0 and PCIe 5.0 and a board power of just 350W. A 512-bit bus will complement 32 GB of GDDR7 memory clocked at 24 Gbps. HDMI 8k is also supported for those that have compatible displays. The card is built using AMD's RDNA2 architecture and a 7nm process (likely TSMC's 7nm EUV).

The gravy train doesn't stop there however because benchmarks of the card internally compared against the estimated performance of a "Next-Gen NVIDIA flagship" were also contained in this internal presentation. I have no idea what this Next-Gen NVIDIA flagship is, but I assume this is based on the leaked specification list for NVIDIA Ampere GPU that was expected to launch this year.

I would seriously advise a grain of salt with any first-party benchmarks, and that applies here too, but based on this leaked slide, AMD's RX Gamma XT is more powerful (at least in these games) than NVIDIA's upcoming flagship. The company seems to be targetting the 4K 120 Hz gaming standard - which was pretty much impossible a year ago. It also goes without saying that this positioning depends a lot on what NVIDIA flagship was used (the to-be RTX 3080 or the RTX 3080 Ti). In other words, wait for third party benchmarks or at least more leaks before deciding.

item.title: An analysis of unclear lessons in video games (2019)
item.description:
For years, we at Ars Technica have discussed how the video game industry might benefit from tearing down its oldest traditions. This November's Death Stranding will ship with a "very easy" mode, and that inspired us to wonder whether other video games should do the same thing. And we loved how the Halo: Master Chief Collection launched in 2014 with an option to hop to any moment within its multi-game library. That prompted us to suggest that more games should let their owners flip to any "page" in a game as they please instead of forcing players to earn their way via challenge.

This week, a gaming and pop-culture critique channel on YouTube looked at the existential question of "video game access" from a wholly different perspective: a year-long analysis of an adult trying video games for the first time in her life. The results, as posted by the channel Razbuten, have been embedded below, and the 20-minute analysis is fascinating on both a macro and micro level.

Even Mario could learn a thing or two

"What Games Are Like For Someone Who Doesn't Play Games," posted by YouTube channel Razbuten.

Instead of calling the video "I made my wife suffer through video games for her first time ever," Razbuten opted for a title that speaks to the inherent learning curve for anyone new to the hobby. "What Games Are Like For Someone Who Doesn't Play Games" came as a result of a full year of the host's wife testing nine video games she'd never played before: Super Mario Bros., Celeste, Shovel Knight, Portal, Doom (2016), The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim, The Last of Us, Uncharted 2, and Dark Souls.

Those nine games were chosen due to their "diverse set of genres and gameplay mechanics, and, well, I liked them," Razbuten said. The host made clear that no guidance would be given to the player. (This was followed by a joke about the couple's marriage ending, along with a quickly stated asterisk of "That's not true.")

While we've seen essays and thinkpieces about the obfuscating "language" of video games and how that can be a barrier for those who didn't grow up with the hobby, Razbuten's video shines because it collects and presents clear video proof of his concerns. It starts with SMB's Level 1-1, often cited as a masterstroke in game design to teach players how to do things like jump, run, face enemies, and collect a power-up. Razbuten's video points to the lack of clear "running" instructions inherent in the act of play, however, while he also examines one way that players could organically misunderstand how the iconic Super Mushroom moves around.

The other side-scrolling games in the test revealed their own awkward moments. In Celeste, the crucial act of air-dashing, required to reach distant ledges, is taught to the player with a seemingly innocuous prompt: press a button and an up-diagonal direction at the same time. As a result, the tester thought this was the only direction she could dash in, until an accidental button tap showed otherwise. And in Shovel Knight, the death condition of having a bag of money appear as a "ghost" confused the tester and left her thinking this was an enemy to be avoided or attacked, not a helpful way to recoup lost items.

Overload

Meanwhile, the tester unsurprisingly got lost in 3D games for various reasons, most commonly due to information overload and unclear onscreen markers. (The video also noted confusion over the fact that, in one computer game, a mouse was needed to move the tester's perspective, which she didn't realize was required for nearly five minutes of play.)

More surprising than that was the tester's issues with a lack of openness and possibility in terms of completing apparent objectives. In Doom 2016, an onscreen marker hints to a required step: walk up to an object, then press the "interact" button to move along. But this test's player saw the onscreen marker on top of a scary-looking object and tried something else: awkwardly kicking an explosive barrel in the direction of said object, then blowing it up. It didn't work.

To be fair, a huge number of genres, particularly puzzle, strategy, and sim games, are missing from this video, as are examples of touchscreen games whose inherent tactile perks may solve some of these concerns by default. Still, the video does an interesting job of selling the tester's familiarity with computers, phones, and technology but not necessarily with the dense, underlying language of how traditional video games work. What's an "L3" button? Do all games have "run" or "sprint" options built in? If something in the game world looks destructible, why can't it be destroyed?

The resulting perspective is an interesting mix of rigid and wide open, and it speaks to how even seemingly "accessible" games can stand to make a little more room for brand-new eyes, especially as services like Apple Arcade, Google Stadia, and Microsoft Project xCloud loom as gaming entry points for people who have otherwise never owned a console.
